findings.reachability: Unauthenticated — triggered by visiting the login page with ?wpcaptcha_unblock=<key>; no session or capability required
findings.justification: Any visitor who knows (or guesses/leaks) the admin-configured global_unblock_key can permanently add their own IP to the plugin whitelist by issuing a GET request to wp-login.php with that key, bypassing all login-protection lockouts forever.

findings.reachability: Unauthenticated — anyone with a valid unblock email link
findings.justification: The transient-based unblock token comparison uses loose == operator (`$unblock_transient == $unblock_key`); while not exploitable via type juggling here because both sides are strings, it is non-constant-time and could theoretically be affected by PHP type coercions in edge cases involving numeric-string transient values.

findings.reachability: Unauthenticated — any user submitting a form protected by the built-in captcha
findings.justification: The built-in captcha stores wp_hash(answer) as a hidden form field; since valid answers are only integers 0-20 (sum/difference of two 0-10 random numbers), an attacker can precompute wp_hash() for all 21 possible values offline and forge a valid token for any captcha challenge, fully bypassing the captcha.

findings.reachability: Unauthenticated — any user triggering captcha verification on login/registration
findings.justification: The sanitized g-recaptcha-response value is directly concatenated into the Google verification URL (`?secret=...&response=<user_value>`); sanitize_text_field() strips tags but not URL metacharacters, so a value like `foo&secret=attacker_key` would inject an additional query parameter and allow substituting the secret key in the outbound verification request.

findings.reachability: Admin-only — requires an administrator to save malicious custom CSS through the plugin settings
findings.justification: The design_custom_css option is output inside a <style> block using esc_html(), which encodes < and > but does NOT prevent CSS injection payloads like `} body { background-image: url(//attacker.com) }` that exfiltrate page data or inject visible content visible to all login page visitors.
